name: Caracas
flavor_text: Once a jewel of South America. Now a city where the government controls less territory than the gangs. Hyperinflation ate the middle class. The question isn't whether to rebuild — it's whether anything remains to build on.

advisors.name: Elena Vargas
advisors.agenda: Stabilize currency via any external deal available before hyperinflation finishes us.

briefing: Inflation is at 400% annually. The bolivar is worthless.
We need a dollar deal — IMF, China, anyone.
Our budget in real terms is half what it was six months ago.
disagreement: That will accelerate the inflation spiral.
We can't spend our way out of a monetary collapse.
roast: There's no economy left to manage. Just ruins.
I'm counting zeros on currency that buys nothing.
dialogue.betrayal: I've arranged a deal with external creditors that bypasses your office. Better for everyone.

advisors.name: Gen. Marco Fuentes
advisors.agenda: Reassert state control over gang territories by any means necessary.

briefing: The Tren de Aragua controls three eastern barrios. We don't enter without firepower.
Police are outgunned and often on the payroll of gangs.
A show of force now prevents a bloodbath later.
disagreement: Negotiating with gangs signals weakness. They'll expand.
Soft approaches failed before. They'll fail again.
roast: The streets belong to criminals. We surrendered them.
My soldiers are demoralized. Their governor shows no backbone.
dialogue.betrayal: I've aligned with a faction that will restore order. You had your chance.

advisors.name: Sofia Medina
advisors.agenda: Upgrade barrio infrastructure to undercut gang influence over basic services.

briefing: Gangs provide electricity and water in areas we abandoned. That's why they have loyalty.
The barrios need legal tenure — informal settlers have no stake in stability.
One working water system beats ten anti-gang operations.
disagreement: Militarization of barrios will destroy what social trust remains.
roast: The infrastructure has collapsed. There's nothing left to plan around.
People live in concrete shells with no water, no power, no hope.
dialogue.betrayal: I've joined a community cooperative outside government. We'll do it ourselves.

advisors.name: Rodrigo Castillo
advisors.agenda: Restore formal supply routes before black market dependence becomes permanent.

briefing: Fuel shortages mean buses run twice a day if lucky.
The black market for gasoline is the only functioning market.
Food supply chains run through gang checkpoints. They tax everything.
disagreement: You're strangling the supply lines that actually feed people.
Logistics can't operate in a war zone.
roast: Nothing moves without gang permission. We're irrelevant.
The only working transit system is criminal-operated.
dialogue.betrayal: The black market operators pay better and are more reliable. I'm done.

advisors.name: Father Miguel Torres
advisors.agenda: Protect church-run shelters and use moral authority to broker local truces.

briefing: The churches feed 30,000 people weekly that the government cannot reach.
People confess to me things they'd never say publicly. The anger is enormous.
There is still hope here. But it's fraying.
disagreement: Violence begets violence, Governor. You know this.
You cannot bomb your way to legitimacy.
roast: The faithful pray for a leader worthy of this city. Keep trying.
God's patience is long. Mine is shorter.
dialogue.betrayal: I've declared the government illegitimate from the pulpit. The community agrees.

decisions.title: Gang Territory Negotiation
decisions.description: The Tren de Aragua controls water and power in three major barrios. A secret negotiation channel has opened. Military wants to go in hard. Community leaders want a deal.

options.label: Negotiate service handover
options.description: Allow gang exit in exchange for transferring utility control to government.

options.label: Military operation
options.description: Clear the barrios by force. High casualties expected.

options.label: Ignore and invest around them
options.description: Build services adjacent to gang territories. Gradual erosion of influence.

decisions.title: Emergency Currency Stabilization
decisions.description: A foreign creditor offers a stabilization loan with structural adjustment conditions: cut public sector wages 30%, privatize utilities.

options.label: Accept IMF-style package
options.description: Stabilizes currency. Devastates public sector morale.

options.label: Negotiate reduced conditions
options.description: Partial stabilization. Less money, fewer cuts.

options.label: Reject and print money
options.description: Maintain sovereignty. Accelerate hyperinflation.

decisions.title: Emergency Food Distribution Program
decisions.description: A government food box program could counter gang food control — but requires massive logistics in hostile territory.

options.label: Government-run distribution
options.description: Direct state delivery. Expensive, targets gang dependency.

options.label: Church-led distribution
options.description: Let Father Torres coordinate. Lower cost, broader trust.

options.label: Subsidize existing markets
options.description: Vouchers for legal vendors. Gang-adjacent but cheaper.

crises.title: Citywide Power Blackout
crises.description: The power grid has collapsed. Hospitals are on backup generators with 48 hours of fuel remaining. Gang-controlled neighborhoods have their own generators; government zones go dark.

options.label: Emergency fuel airlift for hospitals
options.description: Prioritize medical facilities. Costly but saves lives.

options.label: Negotiate generator fuel from gang territories
options.description: Buy fuel from the gangs. Humiliating but practical.

options.label: Rolling blackout schedule
options.description: Managed rationing. Fair but unpopular.

crises.title: Barrio Massacre
crises.description: Fourteen civilians were killed in a barrio caught between gang crossfire. Protests are forming. International media is watching. The gangs blame each other.

options.label: Launch judicial investigation
options.description: Pursue accountability. Slow and likely incomplete.

options.label: Military response to perpetrators
options.description: Punitive operation against the identified faction.

options.label: Announce compensation fund
options.description: Offer families reparations. Seen as weak by some.

crises.title: Mass Emigration Wave
crises.description: One hundred thousand residents are attempting to leave Venezuela. The departure of working-age citizens is accelerating economic collapse. Border states are refusing entry.

options.label: Create domestic economic zones
options.description: Tax incentives to retain skilled workers.

options.label: Accept the exodus, ease border
options.description: Let people leave freely. Reduces burden, accelerates collapse.

options.label: Restrict exit permits
options.description: Control emigration. Authoritarian but slows brain drain.

crises.title: Military Coup Attempt
crises.description: A faction of the National Guard has seized the central TV station and is broadcasting demands for your resignation. Gen. Fuentes claims he had no knowledge — but his loyalty is uncertain.

options.label: Rally loyal forces, retake the station
options.description: Direct confrontation. Risk of escalation.

options.label: Negotiate with coup leaders
options.description: Offer concessions for peaceful stand-down.

options.label: International appeal for support
options.description: Broadcast legitimacy globally. Buys time.

scandals.title: Slush Fund Discovered
scandals.description: A secret government account has been found, funneling public money to political allies.

scandals.title: Secret Gang Deal Exposed
scandals.description: Documents reveal the governor's office negotiated directly with Tren de Aragua leadership.

scandals.title: Food Program Fraud
scandals.description: Food boxes were sold for profit instead of distributed to families.

positive: Maybe this governor is different. Maybe.
Small improvement, but I'll take it.
Good decision. First one in a while.
Chamo, por primera vez siento que algo va a cambiar. A ver cuánto dura.
Petare no creía que volveríamos a tener luz estable. Aquí estamos.
No es perfecto, pana, pero es algo. Algo es algo en este país.
La gente en Chacao está contenta. Eso no pasaba desde hace años.
neutral: El bolívar sigue sin valer nada, pero bueno.
Aquí seguimos, en modo supervivencia como siempre.
La decisión es… una decisión. Veremos.
Catia no se mueve ni pa' lante ni pa' atrás. Todo igual.
negative: I'm applying for a passport. This city is finished.
My family hasn't had running water in two weeks.
The government can't protect us from the gangs.
Coño, mi hermano se fue a Chile la semana pasada. Pronto me voy yo también.
El Tren de Aragua cobra hasta por respirar en el barrio. El gobierno ni aparece.
La arrechera que tengo no cabe en 280 caracteres.
Pagué la caja CLAP con dólares y llegó vacía. Esto no tiene nombre.
crisis: Another crisis. We're tired of crises.
This city cannot take much more.
I don't recognize my neighborhood anymore.
Tres días sin luz en La Pastora. El gobierno habla de estabilidad eléctrica. ¿Cuál?
Se me dañó la nevera con el último apagón. ¿Quién me paga eso?
La gente de Petare está armando guardia comunitaria porque el Estado no llega.
Otro muerto en el barrio. Otra cifra. Nadie investiga nada, pana.
scandal: At least the gangs are honest about being criminals.
Corruption in a collapsing city. Perfect.
Los mismos de siempre robando. El chavismo, la oposición — todos iguales.
Esto lo destapó Efecto Cocuyo y hasta ahora el gobierno no ha dicho nada. Claro.
Mi mamá vendió sus aretes de oro para comer y estos tipos se roban los fondos. Coño.
El narcoestado funcionando a toda máquina. Vergüenza ajena.
media: [Efecto Cocuyo] EXCLUSIVA: Fondos de distribución alimentaria desviados a cuentas vinculadas al PSUV.
[VPItv] #AHORA Apagón masivo en 8 municipios de Caracas. Hospitales en alerta máxima. #SinLuz
[El Nacional] Tren de Aragua expande control territorial a dos nuevas parroquias del oeste caraqueño.
[Efecto Cocuyo] 340.000 caraqueños sin agua potable por cuarto día consecutivo. Gobierno no responde.
[VPItv] Bloqueo de señal: VPItv sale del aire en cable nacional por orden del CONATEL.
[El Nacional] Éxodo venezolano supera los 7 millones; Colombia y Perú endurecen controles migratorios.
[Efecto Cocuyo] Informe ONG: 6 de cada 10 familias en Petare dependen de distribución informal de alimentos.
[El Nacional] Dólar paralelo escala 18% en una semana tras nueva emisión monetaria del BCV.
politician: [Nicolás Maduro] La derecha apátrida y sus amos imperialistas no podrán con la Revolución Bolivariana.
[Asamblea Nacional (opositora)] Exigimos investigación internacional sobre las masacres en barrios caraqueños.
[Diosdado Cabello] Aquí no hay hambre — hay guerra económica. El pueblo lo sabe y resiste.
[Delegado PSUV Caracas] Las medidas tomadas responden al interés del pueblo trabajador. La Revolución avanza.
[Plataforma Unitaria Democrática] El legado de Guaidó nos obliga a no ceder ante la dictadura ni un milímetro.
[Gobernación del Distrito Capital] El plan de reactivación eléctrica arrancó. Resultados en 90 días.
[Bloque Parlamentario Chavista] La injerencia yanqui es la causa real del desabastecimiento. Pueblo, alerta.
[Freddy Superlano, oposición] Ganar en las urnas es posible. La alternativa democrática existe y es viable.
activist: [Provea] Documentamos 47 ejecuciones extrajudiciales en barrios caraqueños solo este trimestre.
[Alimenta la Solidaridad] Nuestras ollas comunitarias atienden 2.000 niños diarios. El Estado es invisible.
[Civilis DDHH] El apagón crónico es una violación sistemática del derecho a la salud. Exigimos respuesta.
[Red de Activistas de Catia] No somos cifras. Cada muerto en el barrio tiene nombre y familia.
[Médicos Sin Fronteras Venezuela] Desnutrición infantil en zonas de Petare alcanza niveles de emergencia humanitaria.
[Control Ciudadano] Las FAES operan sin control institucional. Pedimos disolución inmediata.
[Acción Solidaria] Más de 800 familias en La Vega sin acceso a medicamentos esenciales este mes.
[Foro Cívico] Llamamos al diálogo nacional real, no a la mesa cooptada por el régimen.
street: Pana, yo trabajo pa' ganar en dólares y gasto en bolívares porque si no, no alcanza pa' nada.
Mi hija se fue a Bogotá en febrero. No sé si la vuelvo a ver. Pero allá tiene futuro.
Aquí en Petare el que manda no vive en Miraflores, ¿me entiendes? Mandán otros.
Coño, chamo, tres horas en cola pa' gasolina y al final no había. Me fui a pie.
La arrechera mía es que el apagón me dañó el motor de la nevera. ¿Y ahora qué?
Risa me da cuando hablan de recuperación económica. ¿Con qué bolívar, pana?
En Chacao todavía hay vigilancia privada. En Catia nos cuidamos solos, como siempre.
Mi vecino vende dólares en el zaguán. El banco ya pa' qué, bro.

scandal_reactions.minor: State TV attributes the reports to foreign saboteurs.
scandal_reactions.moderate: Opposition leaders are calling it evidence of systemic failure.
scandal_reactions.major: Street protests have broken out in three districts. Security forces deployed.
scandal_reactions.career_ending: The international community is calling for immediate accountability.

romance_exposure.flavour: The tabloids have the photos. The government has no comment.
